XB4GS9ShBRE.root_cause_summary: The stair traversal and upper-floor exploration are solved by candidate_9's DP9 Phase 2 waypoint pullback (three paused_step resets during ascent → different stair exit position → 280+ exploration steps → DTG=0.131m). candidate_outcomes.candidate_0: Baseline T4. SUCCESS at paused_step=20, floor_step=0 at step 198. Post-switch: only 2 frontiers at 0.2m and 2.2m (DP1 logs steps 199–215). Frontier exhaustion at step 216 → 'In all floors, no unexplored stairs or frontiers found, stopping.' NOQUIT rescues at steps 228 and 241. Five floor_reinits. Episode ends at step 254. never_saw_target. SR=0.7.
candidate_outcomes.candidate_1: Identical to candidate_0 (fingerprint match). MIN_STAIR_STEPS=15 guard never triggered (paused_step=20 already satisfies it). Same step count (254), reinits (5), NOQUIT timing. never_saw_target. SR=0.7.
candidate_outcomes.candidate_4: BFS snap + stale-state fix. SUCCESS at paused_step=20 (same as 0/1). Frontier exhaustion at step 213 (3 steps earlier). Same structural failure. Episode ends at step 251. never_saw_target. SR=0.6 overall.
candidate_outcomes.candidate_8: GUARD_STEPS=35. GUARD_HOLDING from paused_step=12 through 30 (all in_stair_map=False) — 23 extra steps in Phase 2 stair limbo. Agent exits to climb_stair_initialize at floor_step=385, navigate at step 386. false_positive at step ~419. SR=0.6 overall.
candidate_outcomes.candidate_9: DP9 carrot pullback 0.5m toward robot. Phase 2 waypoint changed; paused_step reset 3× during ascent (robot moved >0.2m each time). SUCCESS at paused_step=1 with different stair exit position yielding rich frontier supply. ~280 steps of exploration (steps 199–479). DTG reached 0.131m. Passive detection at step 482 → second stair climb → SUCCESS at paused_step=2 → floor_step=0 at step 491. false_positive at step 499. SR=0.8 overall.
candidate_outcomes.candidate_10: DP9 connected-component validated carrot. XB4GS9ShBRE is NOT a target scene for candidate_10. Behavioral fingerprint identical to candidate_9 (WARNING confirmed). Same paused_step reset pattern during ascent, same DTG=0.131m, same passive detection sequence at step 482, same second floor switch at step 491, same false_positive at step 499. The connected-component check had zero effect here — XB4GS9ShBRE's Phase 2 carrot was already on the robot's navmesh component following candidate_9's pullback. SR=0.8 (unchanged).

ruled_out_reasoning.DP12: Floor switching driven exclusively by 'no frontiers → navigate to unexplored floor' path (step 216 in candidates 0/1) or passive detection (step 482 in candidates 9/10); DP12 50-step interval gate is not the active path in any candidate.
ruled_out_reasoning.premature_stair_climb_success_guard: candidate_1 (guard=15) had zero effect — paused_step=20 already satisfies it. candidate_8 (guard=35) caused GUARD_HOLDING waste resulting in floor_step=385 at stair exit and SR regression. ruled_out_reasoning.post_floor_switch_frontier_seeding_radiu: candidate_9 refutes this: abundant frontiers appear without any seeding change when Phase 2 trajectory changes (2 frontiers in candidates 0/1 → rich supply in candidates 9/10 purely from different stair exit position).
ruled_out_reasoning.DP9_phase2_connected_component_validatio: candidate_10 is byte-identical to candidate_9 (same DTG, same mode sequence, same step count) — the connected-component check found the existing Phase 2 carrot already on the robot's component and returned it unchanged. Zero behavioral effect confirmed. This lever is not on the causal path for the remaining failure (false positive detection).

open_questions: What object was mistakenly detected as 'bed' at step 499 when DTG=0.131m — a nearby sofa, mattress, or pillow that shares visual features with the bed goal class?
Would requiring DTG < 1.5m before allowing STOP prevent the false positive while still permitting true positive stops near the goal?
Is the second floor reached at step 491 the bed's intended floor, or did passive detection send the agent to a third floor where a different object appeared at DTG=0.131m?

Why does the connected-component check not fire for XB4GS9ShBRE even though the scene was not targeted — does the check only run when the centroid is in a disconnected component, and XB4GS9ShBRE's centroid is already on the robot's component?

decision_point_attribution.description: In candidates 9 and 10, the false positive STOP fires at step 499 when DTG=0.131m — the agent reached the bed floor and approached within 13cm but called STOP on a non-bed object. This is the binding failure for these candidates. In candidates 0/1/4, the critical step was 213–216 where frontier exhaustion (only 2 frontiers at 0.2m and 2.2m) terminated exploration after 14–17 post-stair steps due to the stair exit position placing the robot in a frontier-sparse corner.

contrastive_analysis.note: Candidates 0/1/4 fail with never_saw_target (frontier exhaustion at steps 213–216; monotonic paused_step 16→20 indicates minimal Phase 2 movement, landing in frontier-sparse corner). Candidates 8/9/10 fail with false_positive after reaching the bed floor. The bifurcation point is Phase 2 waypoint geometry: a different carrot position changes the stair exit trajectory and frontier supply. candidate_10 is identical to candidate_9 (not targeted), confirming all Phase 2/stair geometry improvements are exhausted and detection quality is the remaining gap.

hypothesis_outcome.hypothesis: candidate_9 improved SR to 0.8 by snapping the Phase 2 carrot toward the robot, but the snap still fails for downstairs centroids where the nearest navigable cell is on a different connected component. Adding connected-component membership verification and resampling from the correct component boundary would fix the remaining failures.
hypothesis_outcome.assessment: XB4GS9ShBRE was not a target scene for candidate_10's hypothesis, so the predicted delta is inapplicable here. The connected-component check had zero observable effect (behavioral fingerprint identical to candidate_9, DTG=0.131m unchanged). The binding failure is a false positive STOP at step 499 — entirely unrelated to DP9 carrot connectivity — and requires a detection confidence or DTG-gating fix.

qyAac8rV8Zk.root_cause_summary: The downstair centroid at [-1.22463054, -8.19236453] lies in a navmesh-disconnected component: pointnav never converges (min_dis_to_downstair oscillates between 160 and 166 pixels across all logged attempts, Reach_stair_centroid remains False on every attempt), and the frontier is disabled twice without any floor switch. Candidate_1's patch targets 'premature_stair_climb_success' — a failure class unrelated to this scene's downstair centroid reachability problem — and produces bit-for-bit identical logs and mode sequences, confirming the patch is entirely off the causal path for this scene.
key_evidence: min_dis_to_downstair oscillates 166→160 in both candidates with no convergence: navmesh boundary prevents pointnav from closing the distance
Reach_stair_centroid: False on every logged stair approach attempt in both candidates — centroid [-1.22463054, -8.19236453] never reached
Frontier [-1.22463054 -8.19236453] disabled twice identically in both candidates: 'disabled due to no movement or reaching start' then 'Pointnav policy stopped. Disabling stair frontier'
Mode sequences IDENTICAL across candidate_0 and candidate_1 ('WARNING: candidates with identical sequences'): get_close_to_stair(164-239) terminates episode in both

stair_mode_runs=1 but tag 'did_not_travel_stairs': mode entered but stair physically not traversed in both candidates
Prior T2 confirmed alternate centroids [-1.26861111, -8.18583333] and [-1.23053279, -8.26782787] are equally inaccessible across T2 candidates 9–14

open_questions: Is the oscillation between min_dis_to_downstair=160 and 166 evidence of the agent reaching the nearest connected navmesh cell but unable to cross into the disconnected stair region?
Does the stair centroid at [-1.22463054, -8.19236453] have a finite geodesic distance from start? If finite, the stair is on the navmesh but the centroid selection targets an off-navmesh sub-cell.
Is there a connected navmesh cell within the stair detection region that could serve as an approach target, and could a persistent centroid failure trigger a spatial search for such a cell?
After N consecutive Reach_stair_centroid:False failures (e.g., 10 steps), could the agent skip to the next floor entirely rather than exhausting all remaining steps in futile approach attempts?

open_questions: Is the couch on the floor accessible via downstairs (climb_direction=2)? candidate_8 confirms the downstairs centroid is reachable (reach_centroid=True confirmed by T5_STAIR_CLIMB_EVAL at paused_step=22–24) — if the couch is on the lower floor, removing or correcting the GUARD for the downstairs case may be the direct solution.

In candidates 9/10, which stair directions do the 2 stair_runs correspond to? Without T5_STAIR_CLIMB_EVAL entries visible in the q3zU7Yy5E5s logs for these candidates, it is unknown whether both attempts are upstairs (Phase 1 fails both times) or whether one is downstairs.

If centroid_bypass_steps is reduced from 8 to 0–1 for disconnected centroids, would the Phase 2 carrot (confirmed on robot's component by candidate_10) enable enough stair traversal attempts within the step budget to achieve floor exit?

candidate_8's GUARD_HOLDING shows the robot exited in_stair_map (in_stair_map=False) and was actively moving (paused_step cycling 0–3) during the downstairs attempt — would removing the GUARD entirely for climb_direction=2 allow SUCCESS to fire on the next in_stair_map=False transition?

decision_point_attribution.description: Step 178 is the last step of explore(83–178) in all 6 candidates before re-entering stair navigation (look_down at step 179). Same-floor frontiers are exhausted and the disconnected upstair centroid is the only known path to the couch's floor. All Phase 2 modifications tested (pullback in candidate_9, connected-component check in candidate_10) produce identical post-step-178 behavior, confirming the bottleneck is Phase 1 centroid approach which no post-step-178 Phase 2 fix can overcome.

contrastive_analysis.note: No successes across any candidate. Candidates 9 and 10 are identical (best performance, DTG=1.045m, stair_runs=2) — candidate_10 proving the Phase 2 carrot was already optimal confirms that all Phase 2 improvements are exhausted. The next lever must address Phase 1 directly: either skip Phase 1 for disconnected centroids, reduce centroid_bypass_steps to near-zero so more Phase 2 budget is available, or fix the downstairs traversal path (candidate_8 shows downstairs centroid IS reachable but GUARD prevents SUCCESS).

hypothesis_outcome.hypothesis: candidate_9 improved SR to 0.8 by pulling the Phase 2 carrot back toward the robot, but the carrot still lands in a disconnected navmesh component for qyAac8rV8Zk and q3zU7Yy5E5s. Adding connected-component membership verification and resampling from the stair bounding box boundary projected onto the navigable floor would fix the remaining failures.
hypothesis_outcome.assessment: Hypothesis is falsified for q3zU7Yy5E5s: candidate_10 is bit-for-bit identical to candidate_9 (DTG=1.045m matched to 16 decimal places, identical DP1 frontier sequence, identical failure tag). The connected-component check confirmed the Phase 2 carrot was already on the robot's component — the mechanism was correctly diagnosed but the target was wrong. The bottleneck is Phase 1 centroid approach (structural navmesh disconnection), not Phase 2 carrot connectivity. All Phase 2 improvement levers are now exhausted; the next lever must address Phase 1 directly.
